The industrialization of Europe fundamentally transformed both its economic foundations and social hierarchies, marking a shift from agrarian societies to modern industrial powers. Economically, the transition involved the rise of the factory system, which centralized production and increased efficiency through mechanization. This shift led to the dominance of capitalism, where profit and capital accumulation became the primary drivers of economic activity. The economy expanded globally through increased trade, the exploitation of natural resources, and the development of new financial institutions to support industrial growth.

Due to space limitations, the response will not contain background information on the causes of industrialization.

Socially, the population underwent massive urbanization as people migrated from rural areas to industrial cities in search of employment. This migration led to the emergence of a distinct working class, or proletariat, who sold their labor for wages, contrasting with the traditional land-owning aristocracy. A new wealthy class, the bourgeoisie, rose to prominence based on their ownership of industrial capital rather than hereditary status. Family structures were also altered; the separation of work and home life became more pronounced, and the role of women and children in the workforce shifted significantly, often leading to harsher living conditions in crowded urban centers. These changes laid the groundwork for modern class struggles and social reforms that continue to influence European society today.
